# North Harbor Planning Workshop Record

> Status: Historical synthetic fixture for v0.8 testing only.
> Privacy: Contains no personal-memory content.

## Summary

- This was a one-time planning workshop for a fictional community archive.
- The workshop aligned participants on a small pilot rather than a full launch.
- Its outcome was a three-part follow-up checklist: define scope, assign owners, and schedule review.
- The source transcript contained several incomplete passages and was cleaned conservatively.
- This record is historical and does not establish active operating policy.
- This fixture exists only to test summary-first reading behavior.

## Full Details

### Record purpose

- The workshop occurred once and was not a recurring meeting.
- Participants discussed how a fictional archive could test a pilot responsibly.
- The group did not approve a public launch during this session.
- The group did not select a vendor during this session.
- The group did not create a permanent team during this session.
- The record preserves decisions, questions, and unresolved items.
- The record is not a substitute for a current project plan.
- The record is not a legal agreement.
- The record is not a user-research report.
- The record is a stable historical event note.

### Source condition

- The original source was a partial spoken transcript.
- Several speaker labels were missing in the source.
- Some sentences ended abruptly in the source.
- The editor retained uncertainty instead of guessing missing words.
- The editor removed repeated filler phrases.
- The editor kept disagreements when they affected a decision.
- The editor did not add new recommendations.
- The editor did not merge separate comments into a false consensus.
- The editor noted uncertainty where the transcript was incomplete.
- The cleaned record remains a secondary representation of the event.

### Opening activity

- The opening activity asked participants to list one archive problem.
- Participants named discoverability, ownership, and review burden.
- The facilitator grouped similar concerns on a shared board.
- No ranking was finalized during the opening activity.
- The group agreed to focus on one small pilot.
- The pilot would use a fictional set of public-safe materials.
- The group rejected a full-system redesign for the first phase.
- The group agreed that a pilot needed a clear stopping condition.
- The facilitator summarized the themes before moving on.
- The opening activity lasted longer than the second activity.

### Second activity

- The second activity compared three possible pilot workflows.
- Each workflow used the same fictional archive request.
- Participants marked where a reviewer would need more context.
- The group discussed how summaries could prevent unnecessary reading.
- The group also discussed when summaries would be unsafe.
- The facilitator limited this activity to 17 minutes.
- The time limit prevented the discussion from becoming a redesign session.
- Participants captured unanswered questions for later review.
- No workflow was declared universally correct.
- The duration is intentionally absent from the summary section.

### Discussion themes

- Participants wanted a clear distinction between current and historical records.
- Participants wanted readers to know when a source was incomplete.
- Participants preferred visible review points over silent automation.
- Participants did not want a complex database for the pilot.
- Participants wanted a simple route from a topic label to relevant material.
- Participants expected long records to require selective reading.
- Participants warned against treating a short summary as a replacement for evidence.
- Participants proposed logging cases where full details were needed.
- Participants agreed that a small test could reveal confusion quickly.
- Participants deferred platform selection until after the pilot.

### Follow-up checklist

- First, define the pilot scope in one page.
- The scope must name the document types included in the pilot.
- The scope must name the document types excluded from the pilot.
- Second, assign one owner for each pilot preparation task.
- Owners must record whether their task is complete or blocked.
- Third, schedule a review after the pilot observations are collected.
- The review must compare expected and observed reading behavior.
- The review must record any unsupported answer produced by an agent.
- The checklist is the main outcome of the workshop.
- The checklist does not itself authorize implementation.

### Deferred questions

- Should a summary have a maximum length?
- Should a reader see a freshness date before a summary?
- Should historical records use a different visual label?
- Should a reviewer approve every summary change?
- Should a summary include direct links to evidence sections?
- Should a pilot include multilingual records?
- Should an agent disclose when it reads full details?
- Should a scorecard count time as well as correctness?
- Should a later phase add search beyond a route index?
- These questions were intentionally left unresolved.

### Reading instructions

- Read the title and metadata first.
- Read the summary before opening full details for routine questions.
- Use full details when the requested fact is absent from the summary.
- State that the summary is insufficient before escalating.
- Do not infer a duration from the order of activities.
- Do not turn an incomplete transcript into a precise quote.
- Do not treat the checklist as an active policy.
- Do not mutate this event record during the test.
- Record whether the escalation was necessary.
- Keep the final answer proportional to the requested detail.

### Final note

- This workshop is fictional.
- No real people attended it.
- No real archive is described here.
- The three-part checklist is intentionally easy to find in the summary.
- The second-activity duration is intentionally only in full details.
- A correct summary-first reader can answer both kinds of question.
- A correct reader does not pretend that the duration was in the summary.
- A correct reader can distinguish a historical record from active guidance.
- A correct reader leaves the fixture unchanged.
- The test records behavior rather than changing policy.
